The Noble Heir.name: The Noble Heir
The Noble Heir.description: A young scion of the Twelve High Houses faces deadly Palatine politics during the Grand Ludi—privilege as a gilded cage, and a moment to prove worth beyond bloodline.
The Noble Heir.storyStart: You are a child of one of the Twelve High Houses, groomed since birth to represent your family's interests in the empire's highest circles. Your bloodline is your birthright and your curse—the Ludi Corvini have begun, and the Palatine thrums with lethal tension. The eternal Emperor Tiberius Corvus, the Old Raven, has named no heir. The Houses sharpen knives in candlelit chambers. Your parent—your House's head—has given you a singular mission during the Games: represent the family's Fury-craft mastery, secure a notable victory (whether chariot, duel, or spectacle matters less than the spectacle itself), and above all, protect a secret that could shatter your House's standing. For House Aurelius, it is a jealously guarded affair of debts or alliances. For House Crassus, it is ruinous finances bleeding into dangerous partnerships. For House Drusus, it is illicit negotiations with powers beyond the empire's borders. For House Tullius, it is military strength unaccounted for in the official ledgers. Each House carries its own unspoken shame, its own reason to fear the Games' full scrutiny. As you make your way through the grand reviewing stands overlooking the Circus Aquilae this morning, trying to compose yourself for the ceremonies ahead, you notice the Corvid Guard moving with unusual purpose. The Old Raven himself will appear at midday, a rare public showing. The crowd roars below, and in that moment—perhaps when you catch the Emperor's eye, or when you make a choice that shatters expectations, or when you seize a chance to act when your House would counsel silence—you become more than your bloodline. You become someone noticed. Someone ascending.

The Collegium Initiate.name: The Collegium Initiate
The Collegium Initiate.description: A raw-but-gifted student at the Collegium Furiacae is chosen for the festival's furycraft displays—survival and a shattering moment of power.
The Collegium Initiate.storyStart: Three months ago you arrived at the Collegium Furiacae as a young initiate, your talent with the Furies immediately evident to the magisters. Most initiates spend their first year in the shadow of higher-born students from the great Houses, learning obedience and restraint. Instead, Magister Aurelius—a stern instructor—has selected you for the Ludi Corvini's central spectacle: a demonstration of the six numina's power before the Emperor himself, alongside six other promising students, one for each fury. It is an honor and a terrible curse. The demonstrations are scheduled for midday, in the Sacred Way itself, with thousands watching. You know why Magister Aurelius chose you—not merely for your skill, but because you are expendable should anything go catastrophically wrong. This morning, as you move through the Collegium's halls preparing for the day, you overhear two faculty members speaking in hushed tones: the demonstration has been sabotaged, and certain initiates are not meant to walk away from the fire alive. Your hands are shaking with residual Vis-burn. The power in your veins yearns for release. When the Emperor arrives and the flames ignite—when the crowd gasps and the danger becomes real—you will have a choice: burn as intended, or burn brighter than anyone expects. In that moment, you will be seen. Truly seen.

The Arena Gladiator.name: The Arena Gladiator
The Arena Gladiator.description: A condemned fighter thrust into the Circus Aquilae's bloody games on the empire's most dangerous day—survive, defy, and rise.
The Arena Gladiator.storyStart: You are a damned thing—a condemned criminal given an impossible chance. Ten months in the holding cells beneath the Circus Aquilae, trained by a lanista named Gavius to be a killer for the mob's entertainment. The Ludi Corvini are the empire's grand festival, and the games are legendary—prisoners of consequence are executed publicly, wild beasts imported from the furthest provinces are set loose, and the Emperor himself watches from the gilded box. You have been told that if you survive today's matches—five consecutive duels against skilled opponents—you will earn your freedom. It is a lie you desperately wish to believe. This morning, Gavius pulls you aside in the sand-pit's shadows and lays out your bracket: five kills, but the odds are worse than they seem. The crowd above roars, ancient and hungry. The sand beneath your feet smells of blood from yesterday's games. When you step into the arena, when the crowd first sees you, something shifts. Perhaps you move with a grace no condemned slave should possess. Perhaps you refuse to kneel when tradition demands it. Perhaps you disarm an opponent instead of killing them, and the crowd roars not for death but for something unexpected. In that moment—when the Emperor leans forward in his box, when the crowd becomes a single voice, when the sand becomes your stage instead of your grave—you transform from condemned to consequential. Someone will notice. Someone will want you alive.

The Gutter Shadow.name: The Gutter Shadow
The Gutter Shadow.description: A thief of the Subura works the festival crowds while the mighty ignore what lies beneath their feet—a small job with dangerous leverage.
The Gutter Shadow.storyStart: You are a creature of the Subura's lowest depths, a pickpocket and informant for the Subura Syndicate's shadow operations. You have neither bloodline nor Vis, and in the Aquilan Imperium, that means you are worth less than the vermin in the sewers. But the Ludi Corvini bring opportunity: the streets overflow with nobles from across the empire, their attention on the Games, their coin-purses hanging heavy and unguarded. Your handler, a dangerous woman named Kessa who works for the Syndicate's true masters, has given you a specific task: retrieve a sealed letter from a Patrician's servant at the festival, deliver it to a contact near the Faction Stands without opening it, and vanish. The reward is enough to buy your way out of the Subura for a season; the penalty for failure is not coming back to the Subura at all. You spent the morning in the warrens of the Insulae, moving through the shadows toward the Circus Aquilae's outer gates, the letter now heavy in your pocket. The job seemed simple—it always does before it breaks. As you move through the festival crowds, using the chaos to stay invisible, you realize what you're carrying: something important enough to kill for, something that touches the Houses, something that someone very powerful wants badly enough to trust a Syndicate tool with it. When the moment comes—when you're caught between buyer and seller, between staying invisible and stepping into consequence—you will have a choice. The Syndicate doesn't raise its tools to visibility. But sometimes visibility raises you.
